retrieved_docs.title: Ear Barotrauma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying etiology of MEBT is inadequate pressure equalization between the middle ear space and the external environment. As per Boyle’s law, an increase in ambient pressure results in a proportional decrease in the gas volume in air-containing body spaces. This increase in ambient pressure can occur when an individual moves down in a column of air (passenger in an aircraft, hyperbaric chamber), a column of water (diver), or due to blunt force trauma or blast injury to the EAC. As ambient pressure (atmospheric or hydrostatic) increases external to the body, it causes pressure to rise in the EAC, while the gas volume in the middle ear space decreases, creating a vacuum. In order to compensate for this decrease in gas volume, equalization of the middle ear space pressure is required. When the middle ear space is not filled sufficiently with air from the nasopharynx, MEBT occurs. As the largest gas volume changes underwater occur near the surface, restricting diving to shallow depths does not prevent MEBT.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Runway Size and Shape Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: A narrow runway may lead to an illusion of being further from the ground, causing the pilot to erroneously feel like he/she should correct by decreasing altitude. This results in a low approach to the runway (increased risk of landing short of the runway or a stall on flare).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Runway Size and Shape Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: Landing to an up-sloping runway may lead to an illusion of a high-altitude approach. The pilot may be inclined to correct by decreasing altitude. This results in a low approach to the runway (increased risk of landing short of the runway or a stall on flare).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Runway Size and Shape Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: A wide runway may lead to an illusion of being closer to the ground than expected, causing the pilot to erroneously feel like he/she should correct by increasing altitude. This results in a high approach (increased risk of a stall or missed approach) to the runway.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Runway Size and Shape Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: Landing to a down-sloping runway may lead to an illusion of a low- altitude approach. The pilot may be inclined to correct by increasing altitude which will result in a high approach (increased risk of a stall or missed approach) to the runway.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Terrain Size and Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: Landing on a runway with up-sloping terrain leading to the final approach may create the illusion of being at a higher elevation than expected. The pilot may be inclined to correct by decreasing the altitude, leading to a low-altitude approach (increased risk of landing short of the runway or a stall on flare).

retrieved_docs.title: Dysbarism -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although underwater diving is the most common cause of dysbarism, dysbaric injury can occur following exposure to any environment with extreme pressure changes. Other examples include high altitude, aircraft cabin decompression, explosions or blasts, outer space, caissons, and tunnel-boring operations.

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Pressure Effects -- Introduction -- Boyle's law
retrieved_docs.content: Boyle's law states that the volume is inversely proportional to the pressure for a given temperature. This law explains why sinuses or the middle ear (normally fixed-volume gas-filled spaces) may hurt during altitude or pressure changes.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Semicircular Canals
retrieved_docs.content: The Graveyard Spin Illusion: Caused when a pilot intentionally or unintentionally enters a spin. Initially, the semicircular canals will register the angular motion, but in a prolonged spin, that sensation will progressively decrease. When the pilot applies the appropriate rudder to stop the spin, the pilot may feel like they are spinning in the opposite direction. This causes conflict between what the pilot feels and what is seen on the flight instruments. If the pilot believes his or her body, they will continue to original spin and lose altitude.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Air Medical Transport -- Issues of Concern -- Characteristics of Air Medical Transport
retrieved_docs.content: Another consideration for both fixed and rotor wing aircraft is physiologic changes due to altitude. Boyle’s law states that the volume of a gas increases when the pressure decreases at a constant temperature. As an aircraft ascends in altitude, there is a proportional decrease in the surrounding atmospheric pressure. At sea level, atmospheric pressure is 14.7 pounds per square inch (psi) (101 kPa). At 10,000 feet, it is 10.1 psi. What this means is as an aircraft ascends, any gasses on board will increase in volume. An example of this is a patient with a pneumothorax could have an increase in the size if they do not have a properly functioning tube thoracostomy. Another issue that has to be considered is equipment that has gas-filled cuffs, such as endotracheal tubes. If the volume inside the cuff is allowed to expand, it could cause damage to the trachea including pressure necrosis. Airplanes are more susceptible to altitude-related problems since they travel at higher altitudes. However, they have pressurized cabins which help correct these problems. Helicopters are still susceptible to altitude-related issues since even at an elevation of 1500 feet, atmospheric pressure decreases to 13.9 psi.

retrieved_docs.title: Claustrophobia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Claustrophobia is a specific phobia where one fears closed spaces ( claustro means closed). Examples of closed spaces are engine rooms, small or locked rooms, cellars, tunnels, elevators, magnetic resonance imaging machines, subway trains, or crowded places. [4] Those with specific phobias generally will report avoidance behaviors regarding the particular object or situation that triggers their fear. The fear can be expressed as a danger of harm, disgust, or experience of physical symptoms in a phobic scenario. [5] It can be unpleasant and distressful; however, most patients find ways to cope by avoiding small or enclosed places. People who react to one of the trigger situations potentially respond to them all. Fear of being trapped, for instance, waiting in a long queue or sitting in a dentist's chair, is also regarded as a sign of claustrophobia.

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Decompression Illness -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: During exposure to high altitudes, ADI may occur through various mechanisms, including the use of high-altitude chambers by the military or improper pressurization of aircraft or flight suits. No specific altitude threshold guarantees the absence of decompression sickness. Commercial aircraft typically have a pressurized cabin; however, failure of that system or noncommercial air travel can lead to decompression sickness. The majority of cases of ADI occur with exposures above 25,000 feet.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology Of Spatial Orientation -- Issues of Concern -- Terrain Size and Constancy Illusions
retrieved_docs.content: Landing to a runway with down-sloping terrain leading to the final approach may create the illusion of being at a lower elevation than expected. The pilot may be inclined to correct by increasing the altitude, leading to a high-altitude approach (increased risk of a stall or missed approach).

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Pressure Effects -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Sudden pressure changes also challenge the air-filled middle ear space, especially the paranasal sinuses. The pressure changes are typically asymptomatic if the sinuses have adequate ventilation through openings or ostia. However, there can be debilitating pain if the ostia are blocked due to mucosal inflammation or other obstructions. If this happens to a pilot, it can lead to dangerous or deadly situations. As noted, gases of a fixed volume contract or expand when increasing or decreasing in altitude. Barotrauma to the sinuses, aerosinusitis, can occur during ascent or descent. On the descent, trapped air in the sinuses creates a relative negative pressure gradient, which causes damage to the sinus mucosa, resulting in edema, worsening obstruction of the sinus ostium, and worsening pain as the descent continues. This is termed sinus squeeze. During the ascent, the expanding gas in the sinuses causes a build-up of pressure that causes worsening pain as the ascension continues. This is termed reverse squeeze. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Decompression Sickness -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Individual factors that can increase a person's DCS risk include dehydration, patent foramen ovale, previous injury, cold ambient temperature, high body fat content, and recent alcohol consumption. Type I DCS, also known as the bends, manifests with skin, lymphatic, or musculoskeletal symptoms and is the most common presentation of this condition. Type II DCS involves the nervous system and is associated with right-to-left shunting of venous bubbles. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Decompression Sickness -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Evacuation by a pressurized aircraft is recommended for patients needing immediate transport to a definitive treatment center. An unpressured air vehicle, such as a helicopter, may be used as long as the altitude is limited to 300 meters or 1000 feet.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, and cervical shortening, third trimester, are both classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that shortening of the cervix at either stage of pregnancy signals a heightened risk of labor onset before term and warrants close monitoring and appropriate management to address threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2403
retrieved_docs.content: Dilation of cervix uteri and vacuum aspiration of products of conception from uterus is a specific technique that falls under the broader category of تفريغ منتجات الحمل المحتجزة (evacuation of retained products of conception). This procedure involves dilating the cervical canal and using vacuum aspiration to remove fetal and placental tissue that remains in the uterus after a pregnancy, thereby achieving complete evacuation of retained pregnancy products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
retrieved_docs.content: Funic presentation, also referred to as cord presentation, is classified as a type of سوء المجيء الجنيني (abnormal fetal presentation). It occurs when the umbilical cord descends into the birth canal ahead of the fetus, increasing the risk of cord compression, fetal distress, and delivery complications. Recognizing funic presentation as a subset of سوء المجيء الجنيني is important for obstetric management, prompting careful monitoring and, when necessary, interventions such as cesarean delivery to protect maternal and neonatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1156
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under the broader group of other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and is recognized as an obstetric abnormality of the cervix. This condition encompasses three specific subtypes: Cervical shortening, second trimester; Cervical shortening, third trimester; and Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these subtypes is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that a shortened cervical length at any stage of pregnancy can herald the onset of labor before term. The unspecified‑trimester subtype is also directly mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, reinforcing its clinical definition. In addition to cervical shortening, Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is another obstetric cervical abnormality, illustrating that multiple cervical pathologies can arise during gestation and the peripartum period. Together, these entities highlight the importance of assessing cervical length and integrity throughout pregnancy, as alterations such as shortening or stenosis are linked to the risk of premature or threatened labor and require careful management to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the third trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, and early or threatened labor is further identified as مخاض كاذب (false labor). This indicates that cervical shortening late in pregnancy is recognized as a sign of a threatened labor episode, which is commonly understood to represent a form of false labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 126
retrieved_docs.content: cervical lesions, fetal size or fluid imbalances, placental hemorrhage, and associated emergency procedures and complications—are recognized as determinants that obstetric clinicians must evaluate to optimize labor management, decide on the safest mode of delivery (including normal vaginal birth or operative intervention), and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are associated with a uterine size for dates discrepancy in women who have a child, indicating that maternal health issues that affect gestation can manifest as mismatches between expected uterine size based on gestational age and the actual uterine measurements in mothers with existing offspring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2172
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function encompasses specific obstetric disorders, including the condition termed “رحم ناقص التوتر” and the condition termed “عطالة رحمية ثانوية”. The clinical scenario described as “Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications” is classified under both “رحم ناقص التوتر” and “عطالة رحمية ثانوية”, indicating that these labor complications often manifest as forms of uterine inertia. In addition, the same set of complications is also categorized as “مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض” (a prolonged first stage of labor) and “مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض” (a prolonged second stage of labor), highlighting that malposition, fetal disproportion, or other labor difficulties can lead to extended labor phases. Recognizing that these entities are interrelated helps clinicians identify underlying uterine functional problems when faced with prolonged labor stages and specific malposition or disproportion issues, guiding appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 763
retrieved_docs.content: and risk factors of pre‑eclampsia, placental disorders, amniotic‑fluid deficiencies, uterine contractile events, and labor‑induction failures with both uncomplicated (normal) and high‑risk obstetric scenarios, including singleton and multiple gestations. Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the second trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, and early or threatened labor is further identified as “مخاض كاذب,” which translates to false labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: Dilatation of cervix for delivery is a specific technique that falls under the broader category of cervical dilation or incision used as a delivery aid, representing a particular method of facilitating childbirth by widening the cervix.
